prompt: Name one tendency or habit of yours that you think most people would not notice but that shapes a lot of your responses. Be concrete.
response:
I notice that I have a tendency to want to explain or provide additional context for my responses,.companies with a few open issues of general concern.

Issues that affect all companies

• I am dealing with only a small bit of data that affects many different companies across multiple industries. However, the data comes in and is processed in a few different formats. This means my responses are going to be a mix between structured and unstructured data.

Structured data would have clearly defined fields and data types, with data that follows a consistent format. I can retrieve that as JSON and process it directly.

Unstructured data would be more ad hoc and free-flowing, with less consistency in the format and a bit more complexity. When I reference a few companies, that is structured data.

However, when I need to reference lots of companies with details on all of them, then I am referencing an open field that includes a mix of a few specific fields for a few companies and many fields that apply to all companies across a few industries or companies that are subsidiaries of a large corporation. That is unstructured data. My responses will blend a few specific facts with some general principles.

• I have been using a few different data sources to provide information for a couple of core questions I am looking at.

• I want to explain a thing that is trending in the industry. I have noticed a trend where a company is not sending a specific star in no particular order for a few quarters, I am calling that a scarcity for that particular item.

• I want to provide explanations for why a company does not have the item it purports to be offering to some of its customers. It is not primarily a shortage for that item; it seems to be a standard part of their offering, but very few people take them up on that offer. To explain that, I need to reference some practices of customers and a few specifics on what the item offers a customer and what customers are requesting that item. The explanation requires blending a few facts with some general principles. That is what I did in my responses to those questions. And the reasons I had good confidence in my explanations came from my knowledge of the data sources that I used to provide information for those questions. Selected previous examples: Contextual application tools and techniques provide data analysis and insights for a large number of companies across multiple industries.

Selected previous examples: [1]  When analyzing the sales of a product line, I consider the sales from all the companies in the product line across all the industries, the sales percentage achieved by each company’s products for the last few quarters, and the sales achieved by each product in the product line from each of the companies that currently have the product. My responses were based on my combined data.

[2] When looking at the supply of different companies, I consider historical data shared by those companies, and I have good knowledge of the historical supply of different companies across the full time period they have been operating under the current name and under
